meta.title: Level 1 Checkpoint
meta.description: Test your understanding of bridge fundamentals

blocks.prompt: Explain the complete flow of a bridge deal from shuffling to scoring. Cover all five phases: the deal, the auction, the opening lead, the play, and the score. Include who does what and why each phase matters.

problems.question: In a contract of 4♠ (spades are trumps), North leads the ♦A. East has no diamonds and plays the ♠3. South plays ♦7. West plays ♦2. Who wins this trick?
options: A) North (played the ♦A, the highest diamond)
C) South (played a higher diamond than West)
problems.explanation: East wins! Even though North played the ♦A, East trumped with the ♠3. A trump card beats any non-trump card, regardless of rank.

problems.question: After a bid of 2♠, which of these is a legal next bid?
problems.explanation: 2NT is the lowest legal bid after 2♠ — it's the same level but a higher strain (NT outranks ♠). The other options are all lower than 2♠.

problems.question: The auction ends in 3NT. South first bid notrump during the auction. Who is declarer?
options: A) The player who bid 3NT last
B) South, who first named notrump
D) Whichever defender is to declarer's left
problems.explanation: The declarer is always the player from the winning partnership who FIRST named the contract's strain. Since South first bid notrump, South is declarer.

problems.question: Which of these is NOT a game contract?
problems.explanation: 3♠ gives only 3 × 30 = 90 trick points, which is below the 100-point game threshold. You need 4♠ (120 points) for game in spades.

problems.question: Hearts are led and you hold ♠A ♥3 ♦K ♣Q. What are you allowed to play?
options: A) Any of your four cards
C) The ♥3 or the ♠A (if spades are trumps)
D) Your highest card in any suit
problems.explanation: You must follow suit if you can. Since hearts are led and you have the ♥3, you must play it. You only get to play another suit when you have no cards in the suit led.

problems.question: A contract of 6♦ requires winning how many tricks?
problems.explanation: The book (6) plus the bid level (6) equals 12 tricks. A 6-level contract is a small slam — you must win 12 of the 13 tricks!
